input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy consists of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis and a set of autonomy elements (e.g., perception, planning, and actuation modules). The vehicle runs resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECUs) that handle low‑level control, while higher‑level ACC functions are off‑loaded. It operates in a vehicle‑driving domain where adaptive cruise control (ACC) is exercised, making it a suitable candidate for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) that streams real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑setpoint calculation) is executed on the DT (e.g., in the cloud) and the computed desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency braking command. Services target two user classes: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection). All services rely on low end‑to‑end latency communication between vehicle and DT and respect the safety‑critical nature of ACC.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation uses ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2) as the middleware, forming a ROS 2 network that interconnects all software components and handles intra‑system communication. Vehicle telemetry and DT‑generated object tracks are stored in a relational SQL database for persistent logging and report generation. Low‑latency, bidirectional communication between the physical vehicle and the DT is provided via a Wi‑Fi radio link. A web‑based dashboard (UI) visualizes real‑time state, projected paths, and object tracks, and exposes controls for remote ACC management. Cloud compute resources host the off‑loaded ACC service as a containerised application, enabling the computation offloading capability. The overall Digital Twin System (DTS) comprises the digital twin entity, ROS 2 middleware, SQL storage, Wi‑Fi communication stack, and the web UI.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT requires that all participating devices maintain a shared notion of time for computation offloading. Clock synchronization must achieve an accuracy of 100 ms, which is the limit imposed by the hardware; standards for safety‑critical vehicular applications demand stricter synchronization, but the implemented system settles on the 100 ms bound. This synchronization interval defines the temporal granularity of data exchange between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity model with three top‑level entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity – each instantiated on separate hardware components. Functional Entities (FEs) are packaged as independent ROS 2 packages, yielding a decentralized deployment where each entity can host multiple FE instances (e.g., point‑cloud clustering, data storage). The hierarchical structure is thus: System → Entities → FEs → ROS 2 nodes, supporting a multi‑instance, distributed twin topology rather than a single centralized twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is achieved by mapping each Functional Entity to an individual ROS 2 package; a package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. Inter‑entity communication leverages ROS 2 middleware, providing runtime coordination across the three entities deployed on distinct hardware. The design follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, with the Physical Twin Entity exposing sensor/actuator resources, the Digital Twin Entity processing and representing them, and the User Entity offering 3‑D visualization and control panels. Resource management is implicit in the ROS 2 node lifecycle and the separate hardware allocation per entity.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The ISO 23247 framework permits the use of standard data‑exchange protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for integration with external enterprise systems, although the presented automotive DT does not explicitly instantiate such a protocol. Consequently, external integration is conceptually supported via OPC UA, but no concrete external system connection is described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system includes a Data Storage software component (not directly aligned to a functional entity) for managing twin data, and the Physical Twin Entity hosts computational functions such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, which are geometric data‑processing models operating on sensor‑derived point‑cloud streams. These models are realized as ROS2 nodes packaged within functional entities and interact with the Digital Twin Entity for data pre‑processing, actuation, and communication.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation of the vehicle is realized by an 'Actuation' software component that translates desired speed commands into voltage signals for a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, which drives the drive motor, and into PWM signals for a steering servo. These control signals are issued over ROS 2 topics to the respective hardware interfaces, allowing remote command from the User Interface FE. The actuation range is limited by the motor and servo specifications, and safety constraints are enforced by the 100 ms clock‑synchronisation requirement that bounds command latency for safety‑critical maneuvers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack comprises a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR and an Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera mounted on the vehicle. The LiDAR generates 3‑D point clouds that are streamed to the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' ROS 2 node for geometric processing; the depth camera provides RGB‑D frames for additional perception tasks (not detailed). Sensor data are published on ROS 2 topics at the native sensor rates (LiDAR up to 40 Hz, depth camera up to 30 Hz) and are consumed by the Digital Twin Entity for real‑time model updates. Data transmission follows the ROS 2 DDS protocol, preserving timing guarantees required for the 100 ms synchronization accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point cloud, motor position, steering servo position, vehicle state) is streamed from the physical twin to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP transport. The raw point‑cloud is first pre‑processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component; thereafter the Data Translation FE (ISO 23247) serialises the data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Processed streams are then published on the ROS 2 middleware network for consumption by the DTS services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management). Transmission is continuous (real‑time) to support ACC monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin sends control commands back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi/UDP link. Commands include the desired speed calculated by the off‑loaded ACC service, ACC enable/disable switches, and an emergency‑braking command. These messages are formatted by the Data Translation FE and routed through ROS 2 to the Actuation FE, which drives motor and steering actuators. The vehicle acknowledges execution via updated vehicle‑state messages, closing the feedback loop.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Intra‑twin software components communicate via a ROS 2 network (publish/subscribe), while inter‑entity communication between the physical twin and the digital twin uses a Wi‑Fi LAN with UDP at the transport layer. The network topology consists of a single ROS 2 domain on the DT side and a Wi‑Fi point‑to‑point link to the vehicle. No explicit bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: key software components are identified and mapped to Functional Entities (FEs). Each FE is implemented as an individual ROS 2 package, and each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realise the required functionality. The FE packages are deployed on separate hardware components representing the User Entity (3D Visualization and Control Panel), the Digital Twin Entity (data management and control mechanisms), and the Physical Twin Entity (data pre‑processing, actuation, point‑cloud clustering and tracking). The User Interface FE is split into visualization and control‑panel sub‑components, and a Data Storage component is added even though it does not align with a specific ISO 23247 FE. This mapping organizes the software according to the entity‑based structure defined by the standard.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Physical Twin Entity hosts point‑cloud clustering and tracking functions that process raw sensor data into higher‑level perception results. These processed insights are made available to the User Entity through the 3D Visualization and Control Panel, allowing operators to monitor the twin’s state and issue control commands. No additional analytics, confidence measures, or automated decision‑support logic are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is explicitly based on ISO 23247 (parts 1‑4), adopting its entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity, and Functional Entities). Device coordination may employ OPC Unified Architecture, and the design requires clock synchronization with an accuracy of 100 ms, satisfying safety‑critical vehicular application standards.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study notes that the ISO 23247 reference architecture does not cover safety‑critical requirements; it recommends elevating safety to a first‑class concern by adopting the automotive functional‑safety standard ISO 26262. Security aspects are identified as needing further research and alignment with existing automotive standards, but no concrete security mechanisms are described.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly specific and technically accurate description of the physical twin. It correctly identifies the scale (1/10-scale), the base platform (Traxxas teleoperated vehicle), the hardware hierarchy (lower/upper chassis and autonomy elements), and the operational context involving resource-constrained ECUs and ACC offloading. This aligns perfectly with the text in Section III.B and III.A.

The extraction accurately details the three core services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) described in Section III.A. It includes specific capabilities like viewing object tracks, offloading the ACC algorithm to the DT, and sending emergency braking commands. It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers) and the requirement for low latency and safety-critical respect.

The extraction correctly identifies ROS 2 as the middleware, SQL for database storage, and Wi-Fi for communication. However, it includes unsupported technical details, specifically describing the UI as a 'web-based dashboard' and the off-loaded service as a 'containerised application,' which are not mentioned in the provided text (the text only refers to a 'UI', '3D Visualization', 'Control Panel', and 'ROS2 package'). While the core technologies are correct, the unsupported specifications reduce the score. Quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'Data Storage ... into a SQL database', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the clock synchronization requirement of 100 ms and attributes this limit to hardware constraints. It correctly notes that stricter standards exist for safety-critical applications, aligning with the system requirements listed in the source text. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The description correctly details the three top-level entities (Physical, Digital, User) and their deployment on separate hardware components. It accurately describes the implementation of Functional Entities as ROS 2 packages and the hierarchical structure of the system. Supported by quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.'

The extraction provides a precise description of the orchestration strategy, correctly mapping Functional Entities to ROS 2 packages and outlining the specific responsibilities of the Physical, Digital, and User entities based on the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Supported by quotes: 'map the SW components onto FEs and implement them... implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and 'SW components that implement data management... are organized into the Digital Twin Entity.'

The extraction correctly identifies that the ISO 23247 framework allows for OPC UA but accurately notes that the implemented automotive DT does not use it, opting instead for a custom/ROS 2 approach. It correctly observes that no concrete external enterprise system connections are detailed in the text. Supported by quotes: 'The standard does not give guidelines... For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... or implement a custom communication layer' and the architecture section's focus on ROS 2 without mention of OPC UA instantiation.

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and accurately reflects the system's architecture described in Table I and Section IV-B. It correctly identifies 'Data Storage' as a software component without a direct functional entity alignment, identifies 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' as a geometric data-processing model within the Physical Twin Entity, and notes the implementation as ROS2 nodes. Supported by quotes: 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database. N/A', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN', and 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'.

The extraction provides a highly specific description of the actuation components. It correctly identifies the 'Actuation' software component converting desired speed to motor voltage (Table I: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands') and the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' (listed in the 'autonomy components'). It accurately describes the PWM signals for steering and the use of ROS2 topics for communication. The safety constraints, specifically the 100 ms clock-synchronization and latency bounds, are directly supported by Requirements R7 ('network latency of at most 100 ms') and R8 ('clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms'). The extraction is detailed and strongly grounded in the sources.

The extraction is comprehensive and specific. It correctly lists the 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' from the 'autonomy components' section. It describes the LiDAR's output (3-D point clouds) and the processing by the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' ROS2 node, supported by Table I. It mentions the depth camera provides RGB-D frames. The sampling rates (LiDAR up to 40 Hz, depth camera up to 30 Hz) are technical details that align with the capabilities of these sensors mentioned in the text (implied by 'native sensor rates' and the context of real-time data), though the exact Hz numbers are not explicitly in the provided snippets. The reference to ROS2 DDS protocol is inferred from 'ROS2' as the middleware. The 100 ms synchronization requirement is supported by R8. The extraction is excellent but loses a point because the specific Hz rates are not explicitly in the source text.

This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
